name: Musculoskeletal Sprains

key_questions: Mechanism of injury: inversion sprain (lateral ankle), fall, overuse?
Ability to weight-bear (Ottawa Rules: cannot bear weight × 4 steps → X-ray)?
Location of pain: bone tenderness (Ottawa Rules) vs ligament?

Ottawa Ankle Rules: bony tenderness at posterior edge or tip of lateral or medial malleolus (distal 6 cm) OR base of 5th metatarsal OR navicular, AND/OR inability to bear weight immediately after injury AND in clinic for 4 steps → X-ray recommended
Ottawa Knee Rules: knee effusion + age ≥55, OR tenderness at head of fibula or isolated patella, OR inability to flex to 90°, OR inability to bear weight × 4 steps → X-ray recommended
Timing since injury: acute (<48h) vs subacute/chronic?
red_flags: Ottawa Rules positive → X-ray to rule out fracture before treating as sprain
Significant swelling + inability to weight bear → possible fracture
Knee: inability to extend knee, joint effusion → ACL/meniscus tear — ortho
Recurrent 'sprain' in same location → ligament laxity, instability
ddx: Septic arthritis (hot, swollen joint + fever — emergency)

first_line.name: Ontario MA Scope Note
first_line.notes: Musculoskeletal sprains and strains ARE in Ontario MA scope (Oct 2023). Pharmacists can assess and prescribe oral and topical NSAIDs.

first_line.name: PEACE & LOVE Protocol + Analgesia
first_line.drugs: Ibuprofen 400–600 mg q6–8h with food × 5–7 days | Naproxen 500 mg BID × 5–7 days | Acetaminophen 650–1000 mg q6h (if NSAID contraindicated)
first_line.notes:
PEACE & LOVE (Dubois & Esculier, BJSM 2019) — replaces POLICE/RICE:
• Protection: relative rest for 48–72h; avoid painful loading; offload if needed
• Elevation: keep injured limb above heart level
• Avoid anti-inflammatories early: NSAIDs and ice may impair tissue healing — limit to first 48–72h for severe pain only; avoid prolonged use; do not use ice/NSAIDs chronically
• Compression: reduces swelling
• Education: active role of patient in recovery; avoid excessive passive treatments
• Load: early progressive loading as pain allows — prevents stiffness
• Optimism: positive expectations improve outcomes
• Vascularization: pain-free cardiovascular activity early (cycling, swimming)
• Exercise: progressive strengthening and proprioception training

NSAID cautions: avoid if eGFR <30, active PUD, third-trimester pregnancy, or on anticoagulants; use with caution in patients >65 years (GI/renal/CV risk). Take with food.

Recovery expectations: Grade I (mild, intact ligament) — 1–2 weeks; Grade II (partial tear) — 3–6 weeks; Grade III (complete tear) — months, may require surgery.
first_line.preferred_when: Acute sprain (grade I–II): PEACE & LOVE + NSAID first 48–72h

first_line.name: Topical NSAID
first_line.drugs: Diclofenac 1.16% gel (Voltaren Emulgel) — apply QID to affected area | Diclofenac 2.32% gel (Voltaren Emulgel Extra Strength) — apply BID to affected area
first_line.notes: Topical diclofenac: equivalent efficacy to oral NSAIDs for local musculoskeletal pain with minimal systemic absorption. Preferred in elderly, renal disease (eGFR 30–60 — use with caution), GI risk. Voltaren Emulgel 1.16%: apply QID. Voltaren Emulgel Extra Strength 2.32%: apply BID — do not apply more frequently.

second_line.name: Compression + Functional Bracing
second_line.drugs: Tensor bandage / elastic support | Lace-up ankle brace (preferred) | Air stirrup brace for Grade II–III
second_line.notes: Compression reduces swelling. Functional lace-up ankle brace preferred over rigid immobilization for ankle sprains — allows early ambulation while protecting ligaments (AAOS recommendation). Grade III (complete tear): ortho referral. DVT risk if immobilized >72h — monitor for calf swelling, warmth, erythema, pain.

non_pharm: Ice for pain relief: 20 min every 2–4 hours for first 48h (not directly on skin — towel wrap); provides analgesia but limited healing benefit — do not use as primary treatment beyond 48–72h
Elevation: keep injured limb above heart level to reduce swelling
Early controlled movement: begin gentle range-of-motion exercises after 24–48h as pain allows
Functional bracing/lace-up brace preferred over immobilization for ankle sprains (AAOS) — promotes earlier return to activity
Proprioception/balance exercises during recovery: essential to prevent re-injury (e.g., single-leg balance, wobble board)
Physiotherapy: for grade II–III sprains — significantly reduces re-injury risk
DVT risk: if immobilized >72h, monitor for signs of deep vein thrombosis (unilateral calf swelling, warmth, redness, pain)
Return to activity progressively — pain is the guide; avoid complete rest beyond initial 48–72h
refer_when: Ottawa Ankle or Knee Rules positive → X-ray to rule out fracture
Complete ligament rupture (Grade III sprain) — orthopaedic assessment
Joint effusion or haemarthrosis — rule out intra-articular injury
Significant functional impairment not improving within 2–3 weeks
Recurrent instability or chronic ligament laxity
Neurovascular compromise (numbness, pallor, absent pulse) — emergency

references: Ontario College of Pharmacists. Minor Ailments: Minor MSK Sprains and Strains. OCP Prescribing Standards, O. Reg. 256/24 (2025).
RxFiles (Saskatoon Health Region). Musculoskeletal Pain — Analgesic Comparison Chart (2022).
AAOS (American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons). Clinical Practice Guideline: Management of Ankle Sprains (2023).
NICE. Sprains and strains: Clinical Knowledge Summary (CKS). National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (2022).
